info.topictitle: 2.1.5 Movements and shifts of the demand curve

markdown:
In the previous section we discussed how **price** and **non-price determinants of demand** have different effects on the **demand curve**:

1. **Price** causes **movements** along the curve
2. **Non-price determinants** causes **shifts **of the curve

### Movements


A **movement along the demand curve **occurs when the price of the good or service changes, and this change affects the **quantity demanded**.

![Figure 1: movement along the demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/f05c49744b5e6d10d26ae2a77ec6dbd1ada43051-2314x1666.png)

The figure above shows the **demand curve** for good $X$ at a price of $P_1$, with a quantity demanded of $Q_1$.

1. If the **price** **decreases **to $P_2$, the **quantity demanded** **increases** to $Q_2$, resulting in an **increase in the quantity demanded** (at $P_2$).
2. This leads to a **movement** **along** **the** **curve** from point $A$ to point $B$.

Conversely:

1. If the **price** **rises** to $P_3$, the **quantity demanded** **decreases** to $Q_3$, resulting in an **decrease in the quantity demanded** (at $P_3$).
2. This leads to a **movement** **along** **the** **curve** from point $A$ to point $C$.

<callout type="note">**Movements** on the curve are caused only by the** change in the price** of the good or service** itself**.</callout>

### Shifts


A **shift of the demand curve**, on the other hand, occurs when a **non-price determinant of demand **changes, altering the demand for the good or service **at every price level**.

<callout type="note">These **non-price determinants** are discussed thoroughly in the previous section (2.1.4)</callout>

![Figure 2: shifts of the demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/8ae3fa5b928be6bcc47a50e7f9ec5514d891c07d-2312x1786.png)

The figure above shows the **demand curves** for a good. Initially, the **demand** **curve** is $D_1$, and the market operates at a price of $P_1$ with a quantity of $Q_1$.

1. If the **demand increases** at every price point (due to any non-price determinant), the curve shifts **to the right**.
2. This is represented by the **shift** from $D_1$ to $D_2$, where the new point $B$ corresponds to the same price $P_1$ but a **higher quantity** of the good ($Q_2$).

Conversely:

1. If the **demand decreases** at every price point (due to any non-price determinant), the curve shifts **to the left**.
2. This is represented by the shift from $D_1$ to $D_3$, where the new point $C$ corresponds to the same price $P_1$ but a **lower quantity** of the good ($Q_3$).

<callout type="warning">Students often confuse **movements along the demand curve **with **shifts of the demand curve**.

Remember:

> **Price changes cause movements along the curve, while non-price determinants cause the demand curve to shift.**</callout>
